caveats: a: The occupation distribution is reported for a broad six-industry durable-goods wholesale aggregate, not NAICS 423620.
a: Observed use of AI is not identical to technically substitutable, not-yet-automated work, and wage weighting is inferred rather than measured here.
a: Current ERP, EDI, and e-commerce automation may already have removed some of the easiest tasks.
rho: The cited sources establish use cases and direction, not a measured implementation probability.
rho: Vendor authorization, data rights, and integration constraints differ sharply by distributor and product line.
e: NAICS classification does not disclose independence, recurring-account intensity, or transferability.
e: The service lens is economically present in distribution but is not separately priced in many invoices.
s5: Gallup covers all employer businesses and owner intentions rather than this industry and completed transfers.
s5: BizBuySell captures only transactions reported through its marketplace and its cash-flow band does not equal the frozen EBITDA band.
q: No source directly measures five-year retention of AI-generated gross benefit in this industry.
q: Retention should differ materially between branded commodity products and differentiated value-added programs.
d5: The demand source is aggregate NAICS 4236 and nominal, while the primitive is six-digit and constant price and quality.
d5: Pandemic inventory cycles and rapid electronics price and mix changes make recent sales a weak long-horizon quantity signal.
o: The estimate concerns continued accountable distribution, not preservation of current headcount or workflows.
o: Large retailers may bypass distributors more readily than small dealers, installers, and hospitality buyers.

envelope.interval_scope: research-input sensitivity with dataset anchors (l, n) held fixed

identity.title: Household Appliances, Electric Housewares, and Consumer Electronics Merchant Wholesalers

lens.customer_and_revenue_model: Recurring and repeat B2B product sales earned through a merchandise gross margin, sometimes supplemented by freight, handling, configuration, warranty, marketing, financing, or other value-added fees.
lens.excluded_activities: Manufacturer-owned sales branches, captive internal distribution, pure commission agents, non-operating product-holding entities, predominantly direct-to-consumer retailers, and firms without transferable customer, vendor, inventory, and operating relationships.
lens.included_activities: Independent US merchant wholesalers repeatedly supplying household appliances, electric housewares, televisions, audio-video equipment, and other consumer electronics to retailers, dealers, installers, hospitality buyers, and other external business customers, including the associated ordering, merchandising, logistics, returns, warranty, credit, and product-support work.

a.bridge: The BLS occupation table measures the broader durable-goods wholesaler group rather than NAICS 423620, while BLS and McKinsey describe AI and online-channel effects on wholesale sales and distribution tasks. I map exposure in sales support, quoting, product-content work, customer service, purchasing, order entry, and administrative work onto the narrower industry, while treating warehouse handling, driving, relationship selling, and exception resolution as less exposed. The broad-industry mix may overstate exposure if appliance distribution is more warehouse-intensive than the group and understate it if consumer-electronics catalog work is unusually digital.
caveats: The occupation distribution is reported for a broad six-industry durable-goods wholesale aggregate, not NAICS 423620.
Observed use of AI is not identical to technically substitutable, not-yet-automated work, and wage weighting is inferred rather than measured here.
Current ERP, EDI, and e-commerce automation may already have removed some of the easiest tasks.
a.change_evidence: A wage-weighted 2026 payroll extract for NAICS 423620 mapped to O*NET tasks, plus time-and-motion samples from inside-sales, order-management, purchasing, and warehouse teams at at least ten independent distributors, would materially change the range.
a.rationale: Durable-goods wholesale employment includes substantial sales, management, office, and financial work alongside material-moving work. Generative AI can draft product content, support quotes and customer inquiries, analyze accounts, and automate portions of order and sales workflows, but much physical logistics and high-context account management remains outside substitution.

d5.bridge: The Census monthly wholesale report measures recent nominal sales for aggregate NAICS 4236, not constant-price, constant-quality demand for NAICS 423620 over five years. Aggregate electrical wholesale sales were materially higher year over year in December 2024, but the series mixes consumer electronics with electrical apparatus and components and is not price-adjusted. I therefore treat it only as evidence against immediate structural collapse and center the five-year quantity ratio near flat; product-price inflation, mix, and stronger component categories make the nominal series likely overstate consumer-appliance quantity growth.
caveats: The demand source is aggregate NAICS 4236 and nominal, while the primitive is six-digit and constant price and quality.
Pandemic inventory cycles and rapid electronics price and mix changes make recent sales a weak long-horizon quantity signal.
d5.change_evidence: Inflation-adjusted five-year shipment forecasts and channel-share data by appliance, housewares, television, audio, and consumer-electronics category, reconciled to NAICS 423620 distributor sell-through, would materially change the ratio.
d5.rationale: Replacement cycles, household formation, retailer inventory needs, hospitality and property upgrades, and continuing product innovation support recurring volume. Direct manufacturer selling, e-commerce, retailer concentration, tariff-driven prices, weak housing turnover, and mature consumer-device categories constrain growth, producing a wide roughly flat range.

caveats: NAICS classification does not disclose independence, recurring-account intensity, or transferability.
The service lens is economically present in distribution but is not separately priced in many invoices.
e.change_evidence: A firm-level census of the 448 dataset-band firms classifying ownership, customer mix, recurring-account share, vendor-authority transferability, DTC share, and standalone operating infrastructure would materially change eligibility.
e.rationale: Most independent merchant wholesalers repeatedly provide procurement, availability, delivery, returns, warranty, credit, and assortment functions to external customers, fitting the repeat-service lens even though revenue is booked primarily as product sales. The haircut removes captive branches, thin import shells, predominantly direct-to-consumer sellers, firms dependent on a single nontransferable authorization, and businesses whose customer and operating relationships would not survive a control transfer.

l.detail: QCEW 2024 private total annual wages at 423620 ($4,232,548,911) x benefits multiplier 1.4294 [ECEC private-industry total comp $46.60/hr / wages $32.60/hr = 1.4294 (March 2026 reference period)] / SUSB 2022 receipts at 423620 ($134,298,919,000). Vintage mismatch: 2024 wages over 2022 receipts, stated. Level: 6-digit. Harmonized to IPS basis ÷1.3407 (median of 65 dual-method codes).

n.detail: margin 0.1137 (margins.json[42]: Damodaran Jan 2026: Retail (Distributors) EBITDA/Sales 11.37%); revenue/employee $2,426,446 (SUSB 2022 receipts/employment at 423620); class <5: 1,172 firms, avg revenue/firm $1,623,452 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $184,587 -> out of $1-10M band; class 5-9: 329 firms, avg revenue/firm $5,927,021 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $673,902 -> out of $1-10M band; class 10-19: 223 firms, avg revenue/firm $10,714,422 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $1,218,230 -> IN $1-10M band; class 20-99: 225 firms, avg revenue/firm $46,974,476 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $5,340,998 -> IN $1-10M band; class 100-499: 87 firms, avg revenue/firm $168,424,839 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $19,149,904 -> out of $1-10M band; class 500+: 56 firms, avg revenue/firm $1,836,334,089 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $208,791,186 -> out of $1-10M band; n_band = 448

caveats: The estimate concerns continued accountable distribution, not preservation of current headcount or workflows.
Large retailers may bypass distributors more readily than small dealers, installers, and hospitality buyers.
o.change_evidence: Category-level 2031 forecasts of manufacturer-direct share, marketplace share, distributor share, customer self-service rates, and fully automated order share, combined with interviews of retailers and manufacturers, would materially change this estimate.
o.rationale: Software and manufacturer-direct channels can remove simple catalog search, replenishment, and order entry, but customers and suppliers still need an accountable operator for inventory ownership, credit, delivery coordination, returns, warranty, assortment, promotions, channel compliance, and exceptions. BLS expects online wholesale sales mostly to complement rather than replace face-to-face selling, supporting continued operator involvement with fewer manual touches.

caveats: No source directly measures five-year retention of AI-generated gross benefit in this industry.
Retention should differ materially between branded commodity products and differentiated value-added programs.
q.change_evidence: Customer-contract and invoice-level cohorts linking realized workflow savings to gross-margin changes, bid outcomes, customer churn, vendor rebates, and price resets at independent NAICS 423620 distributors would materially change the range.
q.rationale: A distributor can initially retain savings through lower overhead, faster quotes, better purchasing, fewer errors, and avoided hires. Retention is limited by transparent product pricing, renewal and bid cycles, powerful retailers and manufacturers, commission incentives, and competitors adopting similar tools. Value-added service, local inventory, credit, and exception handling make immediate full pass-through unlikely.

rho.bridge: McKinsey describes generative-AI applications across distribution rather than five-year implementation rates in NAICS 423620, and BLS expects AI and automation to limit sales-representative growth while online sales mostly complement face-to-face selling. I translate those directional observations into the implemented share of the exposed task opportunity, discounting for fragmented product data, ERP integration, supplier rules, accuracy, cybersecurity, staff adoption, and the continued role of human selling. Because published use cases often reflect larger and more digitized firms, the proxy likely overstates LMM implementation.
caveats: The cited sources establish use cases and direction, not a measured implementation probability.
Vendor authorization, data rights, and integration constraints differ sharply by distributor and product line.
rho.change_evidence: A longitudinal panel of independent appliance and consumer-electronics distributors reporting workflow-level deployment, straight-through-processing rates, error rates, adoption cost, and realized hours avoided through 2031 would materially change this estimate.
rho.rationale: Common sales and order-administration workflows are digitized enough to support implementation within five years, but LMM distributors face legacy systems, thin technical teams, messy catalogs, return and warranty exceptions, and vendor-specific processes. Human approval should remain material where price, credit, compatibility, or channel conflict is at stake.

s5.bridge: Gallup measures succession intentions across US employer businesses, not completed qualifying transfers among eligible NAICS 423620 LMM firms, while BizBuySell reports 28 durable-goods wholesale/distribution sales in 2025 from its marketplace rather than a complete denominator. I use the broad owner-age and intended-transfer evidence to set the opportunity and the observed wholesale sales to confirm that transfers occur, then discount intentions for closures, failed processes, family succession without a qualifying control change, and platform-internal reorganizations. Marketplace undercoverage pushes the estimate upward, while intention-to-completion attrition pushes it downward.
caveats: Gallup covers all employer businesses and owner intentions rather than this industry and completed transfers.
BizBuySell captures only transactions reported through its marketplace and its cash-flow band does not equal the frozen EBITDA band.
s5.change_evidence: A matched five-year panel of NAICS 423620 ownership records, broker mandates, closed control transactions, closures, and family or employee transfers for firms in the EBITDA band would materially change this probability.
s5.rationale: An aging owner base creates a meaningful five-year transfer pool, and observed durable-wholesaler sales demonstrate transactability. However, many small-business exits become closures or informal internal outcomes, and supplier authorizations, working-capital needs, and customer concentration can prevent completion.

principal_driver: Repeat digital sales and order workflows offer plausible AI-enabled productivity while accountable inventory, credit, delivery, and exception ownership remain necessary.
principal_weakness: The business is product-margin based and price-transparent, so a low labor share and competitive pass-through limit the economic benefit retained from automation.
